coredata.dc:title: TBA magnetically functionalized materials combined with biomass-derived fluorescent probe for Listeria monocytogenes detection in a sandwich-like strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Qian
Xiao, Fangbin
Wang, Zhengzheng
Ling, Zhiming
Xu, Hengyi
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasensitive and simultaneous quantitative detection of Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) that can survive in vacuum packaging and frozen food is critical to public health. Herein, teamed boronate affinity (TBA) magnetically functionalized materials combined with biomass fluorescent probe for L. monocytogenes detection in a sandwich-like strategy was established. Firstly, we prepared new TBA magnetically functionalized materials by reducing the pKa value of phenylboronic acid through the TBA strategy in the form of self-assembled intermolecular B-N coordination bonding. To our knowledge, this is the first study where TBA magnetically functionalized materials were utilized for the identification and capture of bacteria, with a high capture efficiency of over 90.8% in spiked lettuce samples for L. monocytogenes (within 35 min), showing superior capture ability. Then, Anti-L. monocytogenes monoclonal antibody was labeled in biomass-derived fluorescent probe with the help of biotin-streptavidin system to achieve specific and sensitive detection of L. monocytogenes. The sandwich-like strategy shows high sensitivity of designed fluorescence sensing platform for L. monocytogenes with a wide linear range (2.2 × 101–2.2 × 106 CFU/mL), a low limit of detection (2.2 × 101 CFU/mL), and a satisfactory recovery rate (85.19%–106.69%) spiked lettuce sample. Notably, the method was low-cost, general and had strong application prospects in the field of food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TBA
Magnetically functionalized materials
Biomass fluorescent probe
L. monocytogenes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Capacity of B-N/APBA@MNPs to capture L. monocytogenes
Optimization of detection conditions for fluorescence biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful to the Research Project of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University (Project No. SKLF-ZZB-202328) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the capture probe. (A) FT-IR spectrum of MNPs and B-N/APBA@MNPs; (B) Hydrodynamic diameter and Zeta potential of MNPs and B-N/APBA@MNPs; (C) SEM images of MNPs, B-N/APBA@MNPs, L. monocytogenes, and L. monocytogenes@B-N/APBA@MNPs.

Characterization of the detection probe. (A) TEM image of CDs (insets: HRTEM image of CDs); (B) Size distribution of CDs; (C) FT-IR spectrum of CDs; (D) Zeta potential of Bio-mAb, CDs, and CDs-SA; (E) XPS survey scan of CDs; (F) C1 s XPS of CDs; (G) N1 s XPS of CDs; (H) O1 s XPS of CDs; (I) UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of CDs (insets: images of CDs under (left) natural light and (right) UV light of 365 nm); (J) emission spectra of CDs in different wavelengths; (K) Fluorescent photostability of CDs at 365 nm UV light and (L) Fluorescent lifetime of CDs.

Optimization of B-N/APBA@MNPs magnetic separation parameters. (A) The amounts of B-N/APBA@MNPs. (B) Incubation time between L. monocytogenes with B-N/APBA@MNPs. (C) Magnetic separation time of L. monocytogenes@ B-N/APBA@MNPs.
Optimization of detection conditions for L. monocytogenes. (A) The dosage of Bio-mAb; (B) Incubation time of Bio-mAb; (C) The volume of CDs-SA and (D) Incubation time of CDs-SA.

Sensitivity of the proposed strategy in spiked samples. (A) The calibration curves of the log C. L. monocytogenes (CFU/mL) with the corresponding fluorescence intensity in spiked PBS; (B) The fluorescence spectrum with different concentrations of L. monocytogenes in spiked PBS ranging from 2.2 × 101 to 2.2 × 106 CFU/mL; (C) The calibration curves of the log C. L. monocytogenes (CFU/mL) with the corresponding fluorescence intensity in spiked lettuce and (D) The fluorescence spectrum with different concentrations of L. monocytogenes in lettuce ranging from 2.2 × 101 to 2.2 × 106 CFU/mL. The dash lines represent the cut-off value (mean + 3SD of the control group).

The specificity results of the sandwich assay were investigated by using (A) fluorescence intensity and (B) fluorescence spectrum. 1: Control; 2: S. aureus CMCC26003; 3: MRSA NCTC13932; 4: B. cereus JX-CDC JDZ102Y; 5: E. coli O157:H7 ATCC43888; 6: S. enteritidis ATCC13076; 7: S. typhimurium ATCC13311; 8: S. flexneri CMCC51572; 9: L. monocytogenes ATCC13932; 10: L. monocytogenes ATCC19115; 11: L. monocytogenes ATCC13932 + S. aureus CMCC26003 + E. coli O157:H7 ATCC43888.

Schematic illustration of the sandwich assay for detection of L. monocytogenes. (A) Preparation of Wulff-type boronic acids and B-N/APBA@MNPs; (B) Preparation of biomass-derived carbon dots and (C) Test principles of the sandwich assay.
Comparison of this work with other methods for the detection of bacteria.

head.ce:title: TBA magnetically functionalized materials combined with biomass-derived fluorescent probe for Listeria monocytogenes detection in a sandwich-like strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330047, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330047, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Institute of Food Innovation Co., Ltd., Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Institute of Food Innovation Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Institute of Food Innovation Co., Ltd., Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330200, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University, 235 Nanjing East Road, Nanchang, 330047, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasensitive and simultaneous quantitative detection of Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) that can survive in vacuum packaging and frozen food is critical to public health. Herein, teamed boronate affinity (TBA) magnetically functionalized materials combined with biomass fluorescent probe for L. monocytogenes detection in a sandwich-like strategy was established. Firstly, we prepared new TBA magnetically functionalized materials by reducing the pKa value of phenylboronic acid through the TBA strategy in the form of self-assembled intermolecular B-N coordination bonding. To our knowledge, this is the first study where TBA magnetically functionalized materials were utilized for the identification and capture of bacteria, with a high capture efficiency of over 90.8% in spiked lettuce samples for L. monocytogenes (within 35 min), showing superior capture ability. Then, Anti-L. monocytogenes monoclonal antibody was labeled in biomass-derived fluorescent probe with the help of biotin-streptavidin system to achieve specific and sensitive detection of L. monocytogenes. The sandwich-like strategy shows high sensitivity of designed fluorescence sensing platform for L. monocytogenes with a wide linear range (2.2 × 101–2.2 × 106 CFU/mL), a low limit of detection (2.2 × 101 CFU/mL), and a satisfactory recovery rate (85.19%–106.69%) spiked lettuce sample. Notably, the method was low-cost, general and had strong application prospects in the field of food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes), “refrigerator killer” (Vidovic, S., Paturi, G., Gupta, S., & Fletcher, G. C. Lifestyle of Listeria monocytogenes and food safety: Emerging listericidal technologies in the food industry. Critical Reviews in Food Science and Nutrition, 1-19. doi:10.1080/10408398.2022.2119205..), is a zoonotic foodborne pathogen of the genus Listeria that can cause listeriosis (Xiao, Wang, et al., 2023). Meningitis and septicemia are frequently caused by invasive listeriosis, with a case fatality rate of about 20%–30% (Wu, et al., 2022). More seriously, more than 90% of human infections with L. monocytogenes are caused by food-induced cross-contamination infections from raw food or ingestion of Listeria-contaminated food that has not been thoroughly heated (Chen, et al., 2023). Therefore, the establishment of rapid, accurate, and sensitive detection methods for monitoring L. monocytogenes in food is important for guaranteeing the security of foods.

To date, several bacterial detection strategies have been constructed. Among them, the traditional culture method is still the gold standard for bacterial detection, but the method suffers from two major shortcomings: time-consuming (>1 day) and labor-intensive, which cannot meet the requirements for rapid detection (Cao, et al., 2024). Molecular biology is limited by the cumbersome process of nucleic acid extraction, enrichment, and purification (Shin, et al., 2022). Additionally, while the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) provides some benefits, including high throughput and selectivity, it also faces challenges with cross-contamination and false-positive results (Zhou, et al., 2023). Instrumental analytical methods need expensive instruments, specialized expertise, and complex operations (Li, et al., 2023). Compared to the above strategies, fluorescent biosensors are friendly candidates for the detection of bacteria, and the inherent properties of the combined nanomaterials strengthen the required analytical parameters for fluorescent sensing (Huang, et al., 2021; Sahoo et al., 2021). Among the many fluorescent labels, carbon dots (CDs) have great potential for application in the analytical field because of high quantum yield (QY), good biocompatibility, high specific surface area and abundant functional groups (Korah, et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2023). For example, Jia et al. achieved the analysis of multiple tetracyclines (TCs) through the internal filtration effect, static burst, and aggregation-induced emission between CDs and TCs (Jia, et al., 2023). Moreover, the use of natural, harmless and renewable materials for the preparation of biomaterials is a current strategic requirement. Biomass waste as a green source of organic carbon is a good precursor for the preparation of CDs.

However, complex components in food samples often interfere with the sensitivity and accuracy of detection methods, necessary sample pretreatment process is expected to improve the analytical sensitivity. Of which, the magnetic separation technology is widely used for target enrichment, with the advantages of easy operation and high enrichment efficiency (Xiao, et al., 2022). Based on this, biorecognition molecules are key to the magnetic separation process, providing the ability to specifically recognize bacteria without binding interfering components in the food sample. Recently, boronic acids and their derivatives have attracted great interest in recognizing and detecting cis-diol-containing biomolecules (Zheng, et al., 2020). Wang et al. utilized 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) combined with colorimetric detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) (Wang, Liu, et al., 2022). The obvious disadvantage of boronate affinity is that it must be carried out in an alkaline medium and may lead to the oxidation of the cis-diol in the compound, which is inconsistent with the pH of foods. Therefore, it is necessary to reduce the binding pH of boric acid materials to be more suitable for applications in real biological samples.

Herein, a sandwich-like strategy was established for highly sensitive quantification of L. monocytogenes in food samples based on teamed boronate affinity (TBA) magnetically functionalized materials and biomass fluorescent probe. Specifically, we prepared Wulff-type boronic acids by the TBA strategy, namely, two ligands of boric acid and amine were self-assembled to form an intermolecular B-N coordination bond to reduce the binding pH of APBA. Subsequently, the Wulff-type boronic acids were loaded on MNPs to magnetically functionalize them and were used as capture probes to achieve efficient separation and enrichment of L. monocytogenes. Ultimately, monoclonal antibody (mAb) modified biomass-derived CDs were combined with L. monocytogenes to form a “sandwich” immune complex structure for dual recognition to improve detection sensitivity. Following magnetic separation, the concentration of L. monocytogenes can be monitored by measuring the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the supernatant. The benefits of the sandwich-like strategy are as follows: Firstly, common phenylboronic acids were modified to Wulff-type boronic acids with improving the affinity. Secondly, the synthesis of CDs with orange peel as a carbon source is easy, environmentally friendly, and inexpensive. Thirdly, biotin-streptavidin system (BAS) coupled the detection probe to L. monocytogenes, reducing the steric hindrance effect and reducing the amount of detection probe while ensuring the coupling efficiency.

The B-N/APBA@MNPs were prepared by carbodiimide reaction in Scheme 1 A. Briefly, MNPs (1.0 mg) were resuspended in 1.0 mL of sterile phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with 1.87 μM 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and 1.50 μM N-Hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (NHSS) based on three times washing. The -COOH on the surface of the MNPs were activated and incubated for 1 h/15 rpm in a rotator, followed by washing three times and resuspending in 0.95 mL of PBS. Meanwhile, APBA (0.5 g) and 1, 6-hexanediamine (0.41 g) were dissolved in 60 mL of tetrahydrofuran solution and reacted at 40 °C for 1 h on a magnetic stirrer to obtain the Wulff-type phenylboronic acid (B-N/APBA) solution. Then, B-N/APBA solution (50 μL) was mixed in the above suspension and incubated for 4 h, which was successfully bound to the surface of MNPs (B-N/APBA@MNPs). Finally, B-N/APBA@MNPs were washed three times, resuspended in 1.0 mL of sterile PBS at a final concentration of 1 mg/mL, and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Biomass-derived CDs were synthesized by the one-step microwave-assisted method according to a previous report (Scheme 1B) (Hu, et al., 2021). Firstly, the Gannan navel orange peel is washed thoroughly with water, dried naturally in the sun, and then ground into powder. Subsequently, powder (1 g) was mixed with 10 mL of ethylene glycol and ultrasound for 30 min to dissolve thoroughly. The solution was heated in a household microwave oven (800 W) for 90 s at the room temperature and the solution became dark brown, implying the manufacture of CDs. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the reaction solution was centrifuged, and membrane filtered to separate large magazines and then used a dialysis membrane (MW 1000) for 24 h to eliminate other small molecules. Finally, the biomass-derived CDs solutions were freeze-dried (−60 °C, 24 h) and stored at 4 °C for subsequent studies.

The procedure of separating L. monocytogenes by B-N/APBA@MNPs was shown in Scheme 1C. B-N/APBA@MNPs (25 μg) and various doses of L. monocytogenes were mixed in 1.0 mL of sterile PBS and incubated for 35 min/37 °C. Then the complexes of L. monocytogenes ∼ B-N/APBA@MNPs were separated via magnet for 4 min and resuspended in 1.0 mL PBS. Lastly, L. monocytogenes ∼ B-N/APBA@MNPs complexes and supernatant were dispersed on polymyxin acriflavine LiCl ceftazidime aesculin mannitol (PALCAM) plates and incubated at 37 °C for 12–18 h, respectively. The following equation is used to calculate the capture efficiency (CE) after counting.
CE (%) = [C+/(C+S)] × 100%
where C is the number of captured bacteria and S is the number of uncaptured bacteria.

L. monocytogenes was examined in actual food and environmental samples to confirm the possibility of the constructed method. Typically, B-N/APBA@MNPs (25 μg) and various doses of L. monocytogenes were mixed in 1.0 mL of sterile PBS and incubated for 35 min/37 °C. Afterwards, the above complexes were separated by the magnet for 4 min and resuspended in 1.0 mL PBS containing 1%(w/v) bull serum albumin (BSA) solution to prevent nonspecific absorption. The L. monocytogenes ∼ B-N/APBA@MNPs were resuspended in 0.2 mL of PBS with 8 μL of biotinylated antibody (Bio-mAb) (50 μg/mL) and incubated for 25 min/37 °C after washing three times with 1 mL of sterile PBS to remove excess BSA. Next, after removing excess unreacted Bio-mAb by the magnet for 3 min, CDs-streptavidin (SA-CDs) (25 μL) was mixed and incubated for 25 min/37 °C, to form sandwich complexes (CDs-SA ∼ Bio-mAb ∼ L. monocytogenes ∼ B-N/APBA@MNPs). Then above complexes obtained by the magnet for 3 min were washed three times and resuspended in 0.2 mL PBS. Finally, sandwich complexes supernatant (180 μL) was read by a Microplate Reader under the optimal excitation wavelength (Ex) and emission wavelength (Em) at 425 nm and 510 nm, respectively.

Phenylboronic acid possesses a broad-spectrum bacterial recognition, and we used anti-L. monocytogenes mAbs to ensure the specificity of the constructed strategy in this study. Therefore, different bacterial strains were used to test the specificity of the proposed method including S. aureus, Methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA), Bacillus cereus (B. cereus), Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7), Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium), Salmonella enteritidis (S. enteritidis) and Shigella flexneri (S. flexneri) were diluted with sterile PBS to a final concentration at 105 CFU/mL. The specific detection procedure is the same as mentioned above.

All experiments were performed in triplicate and data were analyzed by Excel software 2016 (Microsoft Corporation, USA). All Figures were drawn by Origin Pro 2021 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA), Sigma Plot 12.0 (systat software inc, USA) and PowerPoint software 2016 (Microsoft Corporation, USA).

The process for enrichment and fluorescence detection of L. monocytogenes is displayed in Scheme 1. APBA, the common boronic acid, recognizes cis-diol structure-containing glycoproteins in bacteria, such as lipoteichoic acid (LTA) and lipopolysaccharide (Wang, et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2022). However, food samples tend to occur in acidic or neutral conditions, making the affinity recognition of APBA in real samples unsatisfactory. Based on this, we APBA through a TBA strategy to prepare boronic acid ligands with lower binding pH, namely Wulff-type boronic acids (Yan, et al., 2019). With the presence of B-N bonds, the hybridization status of B atoms changes from the trigonal coplanar shape (sp2) to tetragonal boronate anion (sp3). The sp3 hybridization status remains unchanged even under neutral or medium acidic conditions, which is favourable for boronate esterification with cis-diol-containing compounds. As shown in Scheme 1A, -COOH on the surface of MNPs were activated by EDC/NHSS, and capture probes (B-N/APBA@MNPs) were prepared by the carbodiimide method. To achieve the quantification of this method, we prepared functionalized CDs. As depicted in Scheme 1B, we synthesized biomass CDs by microwave-assisted synthesis using a green resource, orange peel. Considering the small particle size of CDs and the specificity of the method, we introduced the BAS system to modify the detection probes on the surface of the target bacteria by anti-L. monocytogenes mAbs and two-step method (Scheme 1C). Finally, fluorescence values were obtained at the Ex and Em at 425 nm and 510 nm, respectively. Moreover, the positive relationship between target bacteria and the fluorescence value can be obtained, realizing the rapid detection of L. monocytogenes.

The preparation of MNPs was verified by dynamic light scattering (DLS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, and scanning electron microscope (SEM). In Fig. 1 A, the FT-IR spectrum of the MNPs and B-N/APBA@MNPs are exhibited. The band at 904 cm−1 represents the plane deformation vibrational band of the meta-disubstituted benzene ring protons (Pan, et al., 2017). An obvious peak around 1601 cm−1 is ascribed to the in-plane flexural vibration of N-H because of the introduction of B-N/APBA (Wang, Liu, et al., 2022). In addition, the FT-IR peaks of B-N/APBA@MNPs at 1636, 1492, and 1451 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of a benzene ring skeleton, the peptide bond and -B(OH)2, respectively (Hu, et al., 2022). Hydrodynamic diameter and Zeta potential data further support the successful preparation of B-N/APBA@MNPs. Not only did the average diameter of B-N/APBA@MNPs increase from 193.4 nm to 220.2 nm, but its surface potential increased to −20 mV after modification by B-N/APBA in Fig. 1B. Meanwhile, SEM images further confirmed the reliability of the results (Fig. 1C). Fig. 1C showed the SEM images of L. monocytogenes and L. monocytogenes ∼ B-N/APBA@MNPs, which verified that the prepared capture probe can capture L. monocytogenes with relatively uniform particle size and good dispersion. These findings prove that B-N/APBA weas successfully modified to the surface of MNPs. Besides, the quantification of B-N/APBA on the surface of MNPs was shown in Fig. S1. We first established a standard curve based on the different concentrations of B-N/APBA in PBS, and the linear regression equation is described as: y = 0.000438x - 0.0177, with the correlation coefficient of 0.9952 (Kur, et al., 2017). Then, the B-N/APBA was conjugated with the carboxyl groups modified MNPs. The B-N/APBA@MNPs complexe was magnetically separated and the unreacted B-N/APBA in the supernatant was determined by UV absorption spectroscopy. The amount of B-N/APBA per mg of MNPs was calculated at 19.84 μg.

CDs were prepared from orange peels by microwave-assisted one-step greening method. And CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, FT-IR spectrum, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. The formation of spherical CDs was confirmed by TEM image and particle distribution histograms with an average particle size of 2.42 ± 0.90 nm (Fig. 2 A and B). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image of CDs (inset of Fig. 2A) also displayed obvious lattice stripes of CDs (0.22 nm), which can be ascribed to the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon (Luo, et al., 2023). Surface functional groups and chemical structures of biomass-derived CDs were explored with the help of FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2C). The band at 3380 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of O-H or N-H (Wang, et al., 2019). The stretching vibration of C-H band appears at 2928 cm−1 (Hu, et al., 2021). The broad peaks around 1738 and 1640 cm−1 are attributed to carboxyl C=O (Chandra, et al., 2017). The peak at 1442 cm−1 is detected for the stretching of C-N (Murugan, et al., 2019). The presence of three bands at 1259, 1084 and 1043 cm−1 confirm the stretching vibration of C-O peaks. The results show that the surface of CDs is rich in functional groups such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, which have good water solubility and biocompatibility. XPS was conducted to analyze the chemical compositions of CDs. Fig. 2E shown CDs is composed of C 52.4%, N 46.4%, and O 1.2%, respectively (inset of Fig. 2E). As shown in Fig. 2F, C-C (284.80 eV) and C-N (286.33 eV) were assigned to the two peaks in the high resolution C 1s. The C-N group is responsible for the peak in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2G) at 400.01 eV. As shown in Fig. 2H, O 1s was further deconvoluted into C-OH/C-O-C (532.80 eV) and C=O (530.80 eV), respectively (Song, et al., 2019). The results of the XPS spectra and FT-IR analysis agree. Besides, Bio-mAb preparation is an essential step for further labeling of CDs-SA. Successful binding of biotin to the antibody was determined by ELISA. The absorbance of the Bio-mAb at 450 nm was higher than that of the antibody alone (Fig. S2) and the surface potential of Bio-mAb was −23.56 mV (Fig. 2D), which indicates successful preparation of the Bio-mAb. Fig. 2D shows that the surface potential of CDs was −26.2 mV, which increased to −21.47 mV after modification with SA, confirming the successful modification of streptavidin (SA) on the surface of CDs. To further prove the ability of CDs-SA to detect L. monocytogenes, CDs-SA ∼ Bio-mAb ∼ L. monocytogenes ∼ B-N/APBA@MNPs was characterized by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). As illustrated in Fig. S3, the green fluorescent dots corresponding to L. monocytogenes surround the black dots corresponding to B-N/APBA@MNPs.

On the other hand, the optical properties of CDs were explored by UV-vis absorption, fluorescent spectrum, fluorescent lifetime, QY, and photostability. The UV-visible light absorption spectrum showed an obvious absorption peak at 250 nm, which was linked to the n–π* transition of C═O and the π–π* transition of C═C (Fig. 2I) (Khoshkalampour, et al., 2023). In Fig. 2I, emission fluorescence spectra under various Ex showed that the optimum Ex and Em were 425 nm and 510 nm, respectively. The photos demonstrate that CDs solution is pale yellow in visible light but fluoresces significantly in green when exposed to 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 2I). Fluorescence emission peaks were red-shifted under varying Ex from 410 nm to 440 nm (Fig. 2J), which was caused by the quantum effect and different emissive trap sites on the surface of CDs (Jia, et al., 2023). The fluorescence of CDs did not change appreciably after 30 min of irradiation with 365 nm UV light, demonstrating that CDs showed high photostability. (Fig. 2K). The fluorescence lifetime of biomass-derived CDs was calculated to be 2.53 ns with a QY of 0.16% in Fig. 2L. Given that orange peel is a by-product of orange processing, the synthesis of CDs from orange peel has the advantages of low toxicity, green, and low cost, which has a broad application prospect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capacity of B-N/APBA@MNPs to capture L. monocytogenes
ce:section.ce:para: Wulff-type phenylboronic acid is realized as a broad-spectrum recognition molecule for the capture of bacteria (Wang, You, et al., 2022), thus we explored the separation properties of Wulff-type phenylboronic acid for common bacteria. As shown in Fig. S5A, the capture efficiencies of B-N/APBA@MNPs for Gram-positive bacteria were significantly better than that for Gram-negative bacteria, which may be caused by the various cis-diol composition and structures on the surface of bacteria (Mikagi, et al., 2022). Meanwhile, we demonstrated that B-N/APBA@MNPs had a good affinity for bacteria by using APBA@MNPs as a comparative candidate. As shown in Fig. S5B, the CE of APBA@MNPs for bacteria were all decreased to different degrees under the optimized conditions, which was also verified in our previous study. In the case of S. aureus, the dosage of B-N/APBA@MNPs was 64.3% lower than that of APBA@MNPs when the CE was 90% or more (Wang, Liu, et al., 2022). Contaminated samples are unlikely to be infected with only one type of bacteria, so interference from non-target bacteria also needs to be considered in the detection process. The CE was at 90% when the concentration of non-target bacteria was the same as that of target bacteria (Fig. S5C), while it decreased when the concentration of non-target bacteria was 10-fold that of target bacteria. Meanwhile, under optimized conditions, the separation performance of the B-N/APBA@MNPs was further evaluated for L. monocytogenes in food and environmental samples. The CE of L. monocytogenes in spiked lettuce and spiked fruit juice were all above 90.8%, with slightly lower in spiked pool water (over 81.2%) (Fig. S5D), indicating the superior separation performance in real samples.

Furthermore, we also investigated the effects of pH and salt content on the separation of L. monocytogenes since the actual sample composition may have an impact on the binding effectiveness. For NaCl concentrations from 200 mM to 1 M, the CE was consistently maintained at approximately 90%, demonstrating the lack of a significant effect of salt on the separation of L. monocytogenes relying on B-N/APBA@MNPs (Fig. S6A). The affinity of phenylboronic acid is strongly influenced by pH (Liu, et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. S6B, the CE remained above 90% when the pH was varied from 4 to 9, proving that the Wulff-type phenylboronic acid can effectively improve the defect that the boric acid affinity must be carried out in alkaline medium. Also, the storage stability of B-N/APBA@MNPs plays a crucial role in the CE of L. monocytogenes. As seen in Fig. S6C, the B-N/APBA@MNPs showed outstanding CE at 35 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for fluorescence biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The assay conditions, including magnetic separation parameters and detection conditions, were optimized to construct rapid and sensitive fluorescence biosensor. On the one hand, the sensitivity of the proposed sandwich assay is significantly influenced by amounts of B-N/APBA@MNPs, incubation time, and magnetic separation time. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the CE of MRSA were increased as the amounts of B-N/APBA@MNPs increased and tends to stabilize when the amount approached 25 μg (97.16% ± 0.48%). It is necessary to minimize detection time while ensuring detection performance. When L. monocytogenes and B-N/APBA@MNPs were incubated at 35 min, the CE reached the maximum (Fig. 3B). Also, the magnetic separation time was optimized as seen in Fig. 3C, resulting in 4 min. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is critical for detection sensitivity. Weak fluorescent signals may result in low signal-to-noise ratios and narrow linear ranges for quantitative analysis. When the dosage of Bio-mAb was 0.40 μg (Fig. 4 A), the incubation time of Bio-mAb was 25 min (Fig. 4B), the volume of CDs-SA was 25 μL (Fig. 4C) and the incubation time of CDs-SA was 25 min (Fig. 4D), the optimal detection sensitivity was acquired.

Under optimal experimental conditions, a series of concentrations of L. monocytogenes was added to determine the sensitivity of the proposed strategy. As shown in Fig. 5 , with an increase of L. monocytogenes concentration (2.2 × 101–2.2 × 106 CFU/mL), the fluorescence intensity was gradually increased in spiked PBS and lettuce, respectively. For quantitative analysis in spiked PBS, the linear relationship of L. monocytogenes was y = 0.12493x + 0.28375 (R2 = 0.9903) and the limit of detection (LOD) was 2.2 × 101 CFU/mL (Fig. 5A). For quantitative analysis in spiked lettuce, the linear relationship of L. monocytogenes was y = 0.10411x + 0.35668 (R2 = 0.9897) and the LOD was 2.2 × 101 CFU/mL (Fig. 5C). Two additional types of samples (spiked fruit juice and pool water) were tested to emphasize the suitability of the proposed strategy. For quantitative analysis in spiked fruit juice, the linear relationship of L. monocytogenes was y = 0.12272x + 0.27129 (R2 = 0.9921) and the LOD was 2.2 × 102 CFU/mL (Fig. S7A). For quantitative analysis in spiked pool water, the linear relationship of L. monocytogenes was y = 0.13914x + 0.17261 (R2 = 0.9697) and the LOD was 2.2 × 102 CFU/mL (Fig. S7B). Moreover, compared with other published methods in Table 1 , The proposed method has the advantages of simple operation, inexpensive, wide detection range, and high sensitivity, which are attributed to the enrichment of concentrated bacteria based on Wulff-type boronate functionalized magnetic nanoparticles and the loading of fluorescent signaling molecules through the BAS system, providing a rapid, highly sensitive monitoring of the pathogenic bacteria.

To validate the specificity of proposed strategy for L. monocytogenes, common bacteria were chosen to test. As indicated in Fig. 6 , the non-target bacteria were consistent with the control group, which had no significant fluorescence signals, while the target group and the mixture group (including target bacteria and others) had apparent fluorescence signals, showing that the mAb of target bacteria ensured the specificity of the proposed strategy. Moreover, we demonstrated the generalizability of the established method by the detection of different species of L. monocytogenes in the same genus.

The stability assessment of proposed strategy for L. monocytogenes in PBS was evaluated using the same batch materials evaluated within one week. In Fig. S8A, there was little change between the same batch of assays one day apart, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.07%, indicating high stability. Recovery tests were carried out to assess the dependability of the fluorescence sensing platform. Different concentrations of L. monocytogenes were randomly spiked according to the above steps and tested three times, and the bacterial concentration was calculated based on the established standard curve of the spiked samples. The results showed a recovery range of 86.19%–113.21% and an RSD range of 6.44%–11.59% in Table S1. In addition, we also compare the proposed strategy with the traditional method, which has comparable sensitivity, but the proposed strategy is less time-consuming (Fig. S8B). All these results demonstrate the potential of the method to be applied in real samples.

In this study, TBA magnetically functionalized materials were used as recognition molecules for the first time, and biomass-derived fluorescent probe with a sandwich structure was constructed for the highly sensitive detection of L. monocytogenes. Wulff-type boronic acids with higher affinity were prepared based on intramolecular interactions between B and N atoms. The synthesis of CDs using orange peel as a carbon source is simple, green, and low-cost, which meets the needs of environmental protection and sustainable development. Even at the concentration of 2.2 × 106 CFU/mL, 90.8% of L. monocytogenes could still be captured in spiked lettuce samples within 35 min by B-N/APBA@MNPs. The sandwich-like strategy was successfully developed to identify L. monocytogenes within 2 h in pure culture and spiked lettuce samples using specific detection probes, and the LOD were both 2.2 × 101 CFU/mL. Moreover, the developed sandwich-like assay provides fluorescence signal readout for qualitative and quantitative analysis with remarkable applicability, simplicity of operation, low cost, and high sensitivity, which achieves a wider detection range to meet the requirement of different levels of the same analyte.

Qian Xu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Fangbin Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Zhengzheng Wang: Formal analysis. Zhiming Ling: Software. Hengyi Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the Research Project of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University (Project No. SKLF-ZZB-202328) for financial support.
